messages.content: Please correct following paragraph: Diffuse large B cell lymphoma (DLBCL) belongs to mature B-cell neoplasms [1] and is the most common subtype of lymphoma [2,3], which accounts for nearly 30-40% of adult non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHLs) globally [4,5]. DLBCL can be divided into two different subtypes: GCB (germinal center B-cell-like) and ABC (activated B-cell-like or non-GCB) according to gene expression patterns [2]. Cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (CHOP) or rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (R-CHOP) are the standard therapeutic settings for DLBCL patients [6]. However, nearly 40% of patients still suffer from relapse or refractory disease [7] and targeted therapies have not been effective in improving survival [8]. Identification of potential therapeutic targets and biomarkers for DLBCL is essential for improving the outcome of current treatments.

Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is a type of mature B-cell neoplasm [1], and it is the most common subtype of lymphoma [2,3]. DLBCL accounts for approximately 30-40% of adult non-Hodgkin lymphomas (NHLs) globally [4,5]. DLBCL can be categorized into two distinct subtypes based on gene expression patterns: GCB (germinal center B-cell-like) and ABC (activated B-cell-like or non-GCB) [2]. The standard therapeutic regimens for DLBCL patients are either cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (CHOP) or rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (R-CHOP) [6]. However, despite these treatments, nearly 40% of patients still experience relapse or have refractory disease [7], and targeted therapies have not proven effective in improving survival [8]. It is crucial to identify potential therapeutic targets and biomarkers for DLBCL in order to enhance the effectiveness of current treatments and improve patient outcomes.

Please correct following paragraph: MicroRNA (miRNA) is a class of small, non-coding RNAs that are approximately 18-25 nucleotides in length [9]. They regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally by binding to complementary target mRNAs and degrade target mRNAs or inhibit mRNA translation [10,11,12]. miR-144/451 is a gene locus encoding two conserved miRNAs: miR-451a (hereafter referred to as miR-451) and miR-144-3p (miR-144) [13]. We and others have previously demonstrated that miR-451 and miR-144 abundantly exist in erythroid cells and play important roles in erythroid homeostasis [14-18]. Interestingly, we also find that 15-18 months old miR-144/451 gene knockout mice develop B-cell lymphomas, acute myeloid leukemia (AML), and multiple benign liver adenomas [19]. These results indicate that miR-144/451 works as a tumor-suppressive gene in non-erythroid cells although the expression levels in non-erythroid cells are very low. Nevertheless, the underlying mechanism is not well defined [19].

MicroRNA (miRNA) is a class of small, non-coding RNAs that are approximately 18-25 nucleotides in length [9]. They post-transcriptionally regulate gene expression by binding to complementary target mRNAs, leading to the degradation of target mRNAs or inhibition of mRNA translation [10,11,12]. The miR-144/451 gene locus encodes two conserved miRNAs: miR-451a (referred to as miR-451) and miR-144-3p (miR-144) [13]. Previously, our research and others' have demonstrated that miR-451 and miR-144 are abundantly present in erythroid cells and play crucial roles in erythroid homeostasis [14-18]. Interestingly, we have also observed that miR-144/451 gene knockout mice aged 15-18 months develop B-cell lymphomas, acute myeloid leukemia (AML), and multiple benign liver adenomas [19]. These findings suggest that miR-144/451 functions as a tumor-suppressive gene in non-erythroid cells, despite its low expression levels in these cells. However, the exact underlying mechanisms remain poorly defined [19].

Please correct following paragraph: miR-451 is predicted to target a large set of genes. In this study, we performed bioinformatic analyses (Lasso regression, univariate, and multivariate analyses) and experiments to reveal that miR-451 plays an important role in DLBCL by regulating fourteen target genes including MMP9, TRIP13, NCKAP1, AQP9, RIN2, EOMES, RAL14, LCP2, SELPLG, MAL, SOCS5, NFIB, S1PR3, and TNFSF13B. The expression of these genes is significantly associated with the prognosis of DLBCL patients. Our findings demonstrate that a specific set of miR-451 target genes affect the outcomes of DLBCL patients and might serve as potential therapeutic targets for DLBCL.

miR-451 has been predicted to target a large set of genes. In this study, we conducted bioinformatic analyses (including Lasso regression, univariate, and multivariate analyses) and laboratory experiments to investigate the role of miR-451 in DLBCL. Through our analyses, we identified fourteen target genes that are regulated by miR-451, namely MMP9, TRIP13, NCKAP1, AQP9, RIN2, EOMES, RAL14, LCP2, SELPLG, MAL, SOCS5, NFIB, S1PR3, and TNFSF13B. The expression levels of these genes were found to be significantly associated with the prognosis of DLBCL patients. These findings highlight the significance of a specific set of miR-451 target genes in influencing the outcomes of DLBCL patients and suggest their potential as therapeutic targets for DLBCL.

Please correct following paragraph:  The GEO dataset, known as Gene Expression Omnibus, includes gene expression data submitted by research institutions worldwide. GSE32918 includes RNA extracted from formalin-fixed, paraffin embedded (FFPE) biopsies of 172 patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) [20]. It is divided into 3 groups: GCB, ABC, and Type-III subtypes. GSE181063 includes RNA extracted from FFPE biopsies of 1311 diagnosed DLBCL [21]. GSE56315 contains human tonsils and tumor tissue [22] (Table S1). GSE32918 and GSE181063 use the Illumina platform, and GSE56315 uses Affymetrix Human Genome U133 Plus 2.0 Array. GSE181063 was used as the training group and GSE32918 as the validation group.

The Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) dataset is a repository of gene expression data contributed by research institutions worldwide. In this study, we utilized three specific datasets. GSE32918 is a dataset consisting of RNA samples extracted from formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) biopsies of 172 patients diagnosed with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) [20]. This dataset is categorized into three subtypes: GCB, ABC, and Type-III. GSE181063 contains RNA samples extracted from FFPE biopsies of 1311 patients diagnosed with DLBCL [21]. Lastly, GSE56315 comprises human tonsil and tumor tissue samples [22] (Table S1). The Illumina platform was used for GSE32918 and GSE181063, while GSE56315 employed the Affymetrix Human Genome U133 Plus 2.0 Array. For the purposes of this study, GSE181063 served as the training group, while GSE32918 served as the validation group.

Please correct following paragraph:  Limma is an R package used for the analysis of microarray data and provides the use of linear models for analysis designed experiments and the assessment of differential expression. The Limma package was used to remove the batch effect between different datasets. Then DESeq2 package was used to select DEGs with P value<0.05 and |log2FC| >3.

limma is an R package commonly employed for the analysis of microarray data, offering the utilization of linear models for designed experiment analysis and the evaluation of differential expression. In this study, the limma package was used to effectively diminish the batch effect observed across various datasets. Next, the DESeq2 package was applied for the identification of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) with a significance threshold of P value<0.05 and |log2FC| >3.

Please correct following paragraph:  To predict miR-451’s potential target genes, several methods were used. ENCORI is an online tool focusing on miRNA-target interactions. TargetScan 8.0 is an online tool predicting miRNA’s potential target genes. Meanwhile, two R packages multiMIR and miRNAtap were used to increase the accuracy of the forecast. A total of 4909 potential target genes were selected for further studies. VennDiagram package was used to search overlap genes between DEGs and miR-451’s potential target genes.

In order to predict the potential target genes of miR-451, multiple methods were employed. ENCORI, an online tool specializing in miRNA-target interactions, was utilized. Additionally, TargetScan 8.0, another online tool known for predicting potential target genes of miRNAs, was employed. To enhance the accuracy of the predictions, two R packages, namely multiMIR and miRNAtap, were also utilized. Subsequently, a total of 4909 potential target genes were selected for further investigation. The VennDiagram package was employed to identify the overlapping genes between the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) and the potential target genes of miR-451.

Please correct following paragraph:  R package ClusterProfile was used to perform Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) enrichment analyses. The biological functions and enriched pathways with p<0.05 were considered statistically significant.

The ClusterProfile R package was utilized to conduct Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) enrichment analyses. In order to determine statistically significant biological functions and enriched pathways, a significance threshold of p<0.05 was applied.

Please correct following paragraph: GSE181063 and GSE32918 datasets contain tumor tissue samples with clinical data. GSE181063 (n=1149) was set as the training group and GSE32918 (n=249) as the validation group. Firstly, the overlapped potential target genes (85) that were up-regulated were analyzed through univariate Cox regression analysis by using the survival R package to screen DEGs that were significantly associated with survival (p<0.05). LASSO (Least absolute shrinkage and selection operator) regression was used to further select hub genes through R package glmnt. Finally, the survminer package and stepwise package were used to include the gained hub genes for multivariate Cox analysis. The risk score was calculated as follows: Risk score=∑_(k=1)^n▒〖(Xk * Yk〗), where X is a coefficient and Y is gene expression level.

The GSE181063 and GSE32918 datasets comprise tumor tissue samples along with corresponding clinical data. GSE181063 (n=1149) was designated as the training group, while GSE32918 (n=249) served as the validation group. Initially, the overlapped potential target genes (85) demonstrating up-regulation were analyzed through univariate Cox regression analysis, utilizing the survival R package. This analysis aimed to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) significantly associated with survival (p<0.05). Subsequently, LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) regression was employed, using the glmnet R package, to further refine the selection of hub genes. The survminer package and stepwise package were subsequently utilized to include the obtained hub genes for multivariate Cox analysis. To calculate the risk score, the following formula was used: Risk score = ∑_(k=1)^n▒(Xk * Yk), where X represents coefficients and Y represents gene expression levels.

Please correct following paragraph:  To further test the model, the model was validated in both the training and validation sets. The risk score formula was used to estimate each sample and divide them into high-risk or low-risk groups through the median risk score. The survival package was used to draw the survival curve and the TimeROC package was used to draw the ROC curve. Risk score distribution, patient survival status, and gene expression heatmap were used to estimate the accuracy of the model. Meanwhile, univariate and multivariate regression analyses were performed to determine the accuracy of the model.

To assess the robustness of the model, validation was conducted in both the training and validation sets. The risk score formula was applied to each sample in order to estimate their individual risk scores. Subsequently, the samples were grouped into high-risk or low-risk categories based on the median risk score. The survival package was utilized to generate survival curves, while the TimeROC package was employed to construct ROC curves. The distribution of risk scores, patient survival status, and a gene expression heatmap were utilized to evaluate the accuracy of the model. Additionally, univariate and multivariate regression analyses were carried out to determine the predictive accuracy of the model.

Please correct following paragraph:  Nomogram is a plot that can visualize the results of Logistic regression or Cox regression. It formulates scoring criteria based on the size of all independent variable regression coefficients, giving each independent variable a score for each value level; For each patient, an overall score can be calculated, and the probability of the timing of the outcome for each patient is calculated by a conversion function between the score and the probability of outcome occurrence. Based on the risk score, a nomogram was established. R package regplot was used to draw nomogram and rms package was used to assess 1-year, 3-year, and 5-year survival.

A nomogram is a graphical representation commonly used to visualize the results of Logistic or Cox regression analyses. It incorporates scoring criteria derived from the magnitude of regression coefficients for all independent variables, assigning a score to each level of the independent variables. By calculating an overall score for each patient, the probability of the occurrence of the outcome at a specific time can be estimated using a conversion function linking the score with the probability. In this study, a nomogram was constructed based on the risk score. The R package 'regplot' was utilized to create the nomogram, and the 'rms' package was employed to evaluate the survival probabilities at 1-year, 3-year, and 5-year intervals.

Please correct following paragraph:  Murine B lymphoma cell 38B9 and human Burkitt’s lymphoma cell Ramos were maintained in RPMI 1640 culture medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator. Human 293T cells were cultured in DMEM with 10% of FBS. Transfection and retroviral infection were performed as described before [15]. Forty-eight hours after transfection, the conditioned medium was collected and subjected to infection of tumor cells.

Murine B lymphoma cell line 38B9 and human Burkitt's lymphoma cell line Ramos were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator. Human 293T cells were maintained in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS. Transfection and retroviral infection procedures were conducted according to previously described methods [15]. After 48 hours of transfection, the culture medium was collected and used for infecting tumor cells.

Please correct following paragraph:  Total RNA from cells was extracted with the Trizol reagent (Cat #9108, TakaRa, Japan). B cells from C57BL6/J mouse bone marrow and spleen were isolated using CD19 MicroBeads (Cat#130-121-301, Miltenyi, Germany) and LS Columns (Cat#130-042-401, Miltenyi, Germany). qPCR SYBR Green Master Mix (Low Rox Plus, Cat#11202ES03, Yeasen, China) was used to quantify mRNA levels according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Mir-XTM miRNA First-Strand Synthesis and TB Green qRT-PCR (Cat#638313, TakaRa, Japan) were used to quantify miRNA levels. Internal control for mRNA detection was small RNA U6 and GAPDH. qRT-PCR was performed on a 7500 real-time PCR instrument (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, USA). The δCt method was used to calculate relative miRNA and mRNA levels. qRT-PCR primer sequences are shown in Table S2.

Total RNA was extracted from cells using the Trizol reagent (Cat #9108, TakaRa, Japan). B cells from C57BL6/J mouse bone marrow and spleen were isolated using CD19 MicroBeads (Cat#130-121-301, Miltenyi, Germany) and LS Columns (Cat#130-042-401, Miltenyi, Germany). To quantify mRNA levels, qPCR SYBR Green Master Mix (Low Rox Plus, Cat#11202ES03, Yeasen, China) was employed following the manufacturer's instructions. miRNA levels were quantified using the Mir-XTM miRNA First-Strand Synthesis and TB Green qRT-PCR (Cat#638313, TakaRa, Japan). The small RNA U6 and GAPDH were used as internal controls for mRNA detection. The qRT-PCR was carried out on a 7500 real-time PCR instrument (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, USA). Relative miRNA and mRNA levels were calculated using the delta Ct method. The primer sequences for qRT-PCR are provided in Table S2.
